title: Ginger warming bath for aching muscles
subtitle: A circulatory stimulant bath for cold, stiff, or aching muscles
excerpt: A concentrated ginger root infusion added to a warm bath. The gingerols and shogaols in ginger stimulate peripheral circulation, creating a warming, flushing sensation in the muscles and joints. A traditional warming bath for fatigue, mild muscular aching, and feeling cold to the bone.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), ginger entry, rubefacient and circulatory stimulant properties, warming action on the periphery. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), ginger as a circulatory herb. Traditional use of ginger baths in both Ayurvedic and western herbal traditions for cold, stiff muscles.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare the ginger concentrate while the bath fills. Fresh ginger keeps 3 weeks in the fridge.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb applied externally that causes localised redness and warmth by drawing blood to the surface of the skin, from the Latin rubefacere, to make red.

content.text: A concentrated ginger root decoction poured into a warm bath. Ginger is one of the herbal tradition's most effective

herbs, the gingerols and shogaols that give ginger its characteristic heat act on the peripheral circulation, drawing blood to the surface of the skin and muscles and creating a sustained warming, flushing sensation in the body. A ginger bath produces a whole-body warmth that lasts for an hour or more after leaving the bath. Maud Grieve notes ginger's rubefacient and circulatory stimulant properties at length; the tradition uses it for cold, stiff muscles, fatigue, and the bone-deep cold that comes with illness or winter damp. The ginger bath is more warming than a plain hot bath and should not be underestimated: a large amount of ginger in the water will make most people sweat.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Skin sensitivity: ginger can cause skin irritation and redness on sensitive skin, especially at higher concentrations. Do a skin test first, add a small amount of the ginger concentrate to a patch of skin (inner arm) and wait 10 minutes. A reddening and warming sensation is normal and expected; burning, hives, or itching suggests high sensitivity and the bath should be used at lower concentration or avoided.

Cardiovascular: a ginger bath is a vasodilating, circulatory-stimulating preparation. People with high blood pressure, heart disease, or circulatory conditions should use ginger baths cautiously and at lower ginger concentrations. Stand up slowly from the bath.

Pregnancy: a full ginger bath is a stimulating preparation and is best avoided during the first trimester. One or two cups of ginger tea is the appropriate pregnancy preparation.

Open skin: do not use ginger baths on broken, cut, or inflamed skin.

items.prepNote: fresh, unpeeled, about a 10 cm piece, sliced or roughly grated
for the concentrate, which will then be added to the full bath

content.text: Slice or roughly grate {{ginger-root}} of fresh ginger. Place in the large saucepan with {{water}} of water. Bring to a simmer, lid the pan, and simmer gently for 15 to 20 minutes. The water will turn pale yellow and smell strongly of ginger. Strain through a sieve into a jug.

Run a warm bath, not too hot. Pour the ginger concentrate into the bath and swirl to mix. Step in slowly. The ginger sensation builds gradually over the first five minutes, an increasing warmth and reddening of the skin. Soak for 15 to 20 minutes. Stand up slowly when leaving the bath.

One ginger bath as needed for muscular aching, fatigue, or cold. Can be repeated nightly during a period of intense physical use or illness. The warming effect typically lasts one to two hours post-bath. Rest afterwards rather than immediately going out in the cold.
When NOT to use this remedy

Avoid ginger baths on broken skin. Use with caution if you have high blood pressure, heart disease, or circulatory conditions. Avoid in the first trimester of pregnancy. See your doctor for muscular pain that is severe, unexplained, or accompanied by swelling, weakness, or neurological symptoms, ginger baths are for mild post-exertion aching, not for injury or inflammatory conditions.
